coredata.dc:title: A novel nanobiosorbent of functionalized graphene quantum dots from rice husk with barium hydroxide for microwave enhanced removal of lead (II) and lanthanum (III)
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
Fekry, Nesma A.
Abdelfattah, Amir M.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, rice husk was used as a sustainable source to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDOs) with 2D morphology. Chemical modification of GQDOs with Ba(OH)2 was followed to form a novel GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent with an increased number of surface hydroxyl groups. The physicochemical properties of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba were investigated by FT-IR, SEM, TEM, TGA, and XRD. The adsorption parameters of Pb(II) and La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba were optimized using microwave sorption approach. The maximum capacity reached 3400 µmol g−1 (pH 7), and 1500 µmol g−1 (pH 5) at 15 s for Pb(II) and La(III), respectively. The adsorption isotherm models by GQDOs-Ba fitted well with Langmuir. The pseudo-second order was agreed by Pb(II) and La(III) ions. The thermodynamic studies elucidated that Pb(II) and La(III) adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba followed a spontaneous model. The GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent accomplished excellent removal percentages from different water samples containing lead (98.5%–99.8%) and lanthanum (94.6%–96.2%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Rice husk
Barium hydroxide
Removal
Lead (II) and lanthanum (III)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sorption studies of Pb(II) and La(III) by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent
Sorption studies of Pb(II) and La(III) by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent
Effect of reaction time and kinetics models
Effect of concentration and adsorption isotherm models
Effect of reaction temperature and thermodynamic study
Applications for extraction of Pb(II) and La(III) from real water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impact of mass dosage of GQDOs-Ba on Pb(II) and La(III) removal.
(a) Effect of contact time and (b) pseudo-second order.
Langmuir isotherm for Pb(II) and La(III).
Effect of interfering on Pb(II) and La(III) removal.
Effect of pH on sorption capacity values.
Kinetic parameters for sorption of lead and lanthanum ions.*

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: qe (mg g−1) is the amount of Pb(II) or La(III) adsorbed onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent at equilibrium, K1 (min−1) is the first-order-rate constant (g mg−1 min−1), K2 is the second-order-rate constant (g mg−1 min−1), Kid is the intra-particle diffusion rate constant (mg g−1 min−1/2), C is a constant (mg g−1), α is the initial adsorption rate of Pb(II) or La(II) onto GQDOs-Ba (mg g−1 min−1) and β is related to the extent of surface coverage and the activation energy for chemisorption (g mg−1).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Isotherm Parameters for sorption of lead and lanthanum ions.*

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: qmax (the greatest amount of Pb(II) or La(III) per a unit weight of GQDOs-Ba as nanobiosorbent, mg g−1), b (the Langmuir isotherm constant, L mg−1),), Kf (the Freundlich isotherm constant, L mg−1), 1/n (the heterogeneity factor, limited in the range 0 to 1), bT (Temkin isotherm consistent), AT (the Temkin equilibrium constant, L g−1), Kads gives Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm constant (mol2/kJ2).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of temperature on sorption capacity values.*

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ΔG°: Gibbs free energy change, ΔH°: enthalpy, ΔS°: entropy.

head.ce:title: A novel nanobiosorbent of functionalized graphene quantum dots from rice husk with barium hydroxide for microwave enhanced removal of lead (II) and lanthanum (III)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Moharram Bey, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, P.O. Box 426, Ibrahimia, 21321 Alexandria, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, rice husk was used as a sustainable source to synthesize graphene quantum dots (GQDOs) with 2D morphology. Chemical modification of GQDOs with Ba(OH)2 was followed to form a novel GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent with an increased number of surface hydroxyl groups. The physicochemical properties of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba were investigated by FT-IR, SEM, TEM, TGA, and XRD. The adsorption parameters of Pb(II) and La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba were optimized using microwave sorption approach. The maximum capacity reached 3400 µmol g−1 (pH 7), and 1500 µmol g−1 (pH 5) at 15 s for Pb(II) and La(III), respectively. The adsorption isotherm models by GQDOs-Ba fitted well with Langmuir. The pseudo-second order was agreed by Pb(II) and La(III) ions. The thermodynamic studies elucidated that Pb(II) and La(III) adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba followed a spontaneous model. The GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent accomplished excellent removal percentages from different water samples containing lead (98.5%–99.8%) and lanthanum (94.6%–96.2%).

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing the number of technological and industrial applications over the previous decades has significantly participated in more polluted water. A good number of industrial applications are developing by utilizing heavy metal ions and rare earth elements (REEs), and simultaneously contribute to the environmental contamination (El-Taher, 2007). As some heavy metals are possible risky to human healthiness, attention is being assumed to its routes of entry either directly or indirectly into the human nourishment chain (Abdel-Haleem et al., 2001). Lead is one of the major important metals with high interest in some industries such as battery manufacturing, finishing, painting, and other industries. Lead is non-biodegradable and inclines to bio-accumulates in living systems. Toxicity of lead may cause nervous breakdown, destroy the liver, kidney, and brain. According to USEPA, the ultimate concentrated level for lead was listed as 0.05 mg L−1 and the target of concentrated lead is zero (Gupta et al, 2011; Mahmoud et al., 2020).

Recently, REEs have gotten huge worries with their increasing demands in Hi-Tech manufactures for their special characteristics. Lanthanum (La(III)) is one of the major REEs utilized in various application fields as cutting edge new materials, for example, catalysts, steel, special ceramics, and optical glasses (Wu et al., 2010). La(III) is considered as a fission item from nuclear power reactors, it might be leaked to the earth and cause risks. It is considered as a dangerous material because of the genotoxicity for human blood lymphocytes (El-Sofany, 2008). Consequently, the extraction of heavy metals and REEs containing wastes is significant for actual ecological contaminant cleanup. Numerous advances have been progressed for removal of toxic species, for example, precipitation, membrane filtration, liquid–liquid extraction (L-L), adsorption, ion exchange and others (Tran et al., 2017; Periyasamy et al., 2017). Amongst these techniques, adsorption was believed as one of the major well-known technology owing to its simple operation, low-cost treatment and practical enforcement (Mahmoud and Fekry, 2018; Yao et al., 2018). Adsorption technique is mainly favored due to its most preferred performance along with the outcomes of its effectiveness and simplicity (Gao et al., 2014: Shreadah et al., 2019).

Carbon-based graphene materials have been viewed as incredibly useful sorbents for removal of heavy metals owing to the possible binding with oxygen, hydroxyl, carboxylic and epoxy functional groups which coordinate with such pollutants (Chai et al., 2019; Zhou et al. 2019; Lai et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2018). In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDOs) have attracted much attention for their promising applications, especially photocatalysis (Hu et al., 2016; Safardoust-Hojaghan and Salavati-Niasari, 2017), biological labeling, heavy metals removal, and biomedicine (Zhuo et al., 2012). In addition, GQDOs have distinct electrical and optical properties due to the quantum retention effect (Peng et al., 2012; Ponomarenko et al., 2008). Recently, various precursors were used to preparing GQDOs such as graphite oxide (Dey et al., 2014), citric acid (Hasanzadeh et al., 2016), and rice husk (Dong et al., 2012). In comparison with other nanomaterials, GQDOs were found and listed as low cost, ease of functionalization and low toxicity (Dey et al., 2014). Furthermore, the surface of the GQDOs was filled with a superior number of functional groups such as carboxylic, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups with the capability of catching positively ions from solutions (Wang et al., 2016; Xiaoyan et al, 2016).

Rice husk (RH) which is a by-product of the rice milling process and generally produced at least one million tons every year in Egypt (El Sharkawi et al., 2014). Unfortunately, most of this material is burnt in outdoors leading to grievous atmospheric problems, regardless of the fact that low amounts are consumed in domestic uses (Fierro et al., 2010). Thus, the utilization of RH in multidiscipline applications may come with some benefits with respect to the low cost and environmental protection. RH was used as a pioneering material for many industrial applications due to its native availability at virtually no cost, chemical stability, and high mechanical force (Awang et al., 2009). Moreover, RH contains organic materials (75–90%), ash (17–20%) and inorganic materials containing silicon dioxide (Cheng, et al., 2019). So, RH has been commonly implemented as a potential precursor for the production of GQDOs (De Luna et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2016).

In the current research, RH bi-product has been used as a low-cost material to obtain GQDOs with some functionalities such as carboxylic (–COOH) and hydroxyl groups (–OH). The produced GQDOs were additionally treated with barium hydroxide (GQDOs-Ba) to improve its binding separation characteristics from aqueous solution. The FT-IR, SEM, TEM, TGA and XRD techniques were applied to deeply investigate the synthesized GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The GQDOs-Ba were examined to optimize the ultrafast adsorption of Pb(II) and La(III) from aqueous solution using microwave sorption technique. Kinetics and thermodynamic modeling studies were used to interpret the adsorption mechanisms of Pb(II) and La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba.

Rice husk was collected from Albehra, Egypt. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, FW 40, assay 98%) was purchased from SDFCL (SD fine Chem limited), India. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, FW 98.08, assay 99.99%) and nitric acid (HNO3, FW 63.01, assay 65%) were from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company, USA. Lanthanum oxide (La2O3, F.W 325.81, assay 99%) was bought from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company, USA. Lead acetate trihydrate (Pb(CH3COO)2·3H2O FW 379.33, assay 99.99%), barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2·2H2O, FW 244.27, assay 99.0%), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, FW 53.49, assay 99.8) and potassium chloride (KCl, FW 74.55, assay 99.5%) were supplied from BDH, UK. Sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, FW 142.04, assay 99.0–100.5%) was purchased from Riedel de HaËn, Germany. Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (MgSO4·7H2O, FW 246.48, assay 99%) and calcium sulfate (CaSO4, FW 136.14, assay 98%) were purchased from Egypt chemical company. Ethylenediaminetetraacetate disodium dihydrate (EDTA) (C10H14N2Na2O8·2H2O, FW 372.24, assay 99.0–101.0%) from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company, USA was utilized to obtain the concentration of Pb(II) and La(III) using the applicable buffer and indicator.

Surface functionalization of GQDOs to form GQDOs-Ba was investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FTIR, BRUKER Vertex 70). The size and morphology of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba were observed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM-JEM-2100 plus, operated at 10KV) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The thermal stability of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba were measured by the thermogravimetric analyzer (SHIMADZU TGA-50). In addition, the samples of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu lab X6100, 40 kV and 30 mA) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1 nm). The fluorescent spectrum of GQDOs was carried out with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Parkin-Elmer ls55). The UV–Vis spectrum was obtained on A UV- spectrophotometer (Unico, 7200). In the application part, the concentration (mg L−1) was examined by inductively coupled plasma (ICP, Varian 710). The microwave sorption technique was investigated by the microwave oven (Smart, SM-3025MS, 900 W, 2.45GHZ).

A bottom-up method was used for the preparation of GQDOs from organic components in rice husk using two stages. Firstly, RH was converted to carbon by the following procedure. RH was thoroughly rinsed with distilled water and dried at 110 °C for 24 h in an oven. The RH was then treated with N2 atmosphere at 700 °C for 2 h. The product ash contains both silica and carbon. A solution of sodium hydroxide (1 mol L−1) was added to the RH ash and boiled for 1 h with vigorous stirring to convert it into a mixture of sodium silicate and carbon. The carbon residues were filtered and rinsed with 150 mL hot water. The carbon sample was dried at 80 °C overnight. Secondly, carbon was used to synthesize GQDOs by refluxing with concentrated acids (HNO3 and H2SO4). In a typical method, 0.2 g synthesized carbon was placed into 50 mL HNO3 (65%) and 50 mL H2SO4 (98.08%) followed by refluxing for 24 h. After cooling, the brown colloidal GQDOs were centrifuged for twenty min to obtain the required material. The sediment was heated at 200 °C to evaporate water and acids and finally, a reddish-brown solid was produced. Fluorescence properties of GQDOs were detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. An aqueous solution of GQDOs is light brown under visible light, but GQDOs have fluorescence under the excitation of a beam of 360 nm. The maximum emission wavelength of GQDOs was stable at 530 nm during the excitation wavelengths (lannazzo et al., 2017, Ye et al., 2013).

The GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was synthesized using in situ precipitation of barium hydroxide with GQDOs. In this procedure, the brown suspension of GQDOs was adjusted to pH 8.0 by dropwise addition of concentrated NaOH and continuous stirring. Furthermore, a solution of BaCl2 (5 mol L−1) was added dropwise until a grey precipitate was separated and a clear solution above it was formed as shown as Scheme 1 . Finally, the precipitated GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was filtered, rinsed with hot distilled water and dried at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorption studies of Pb(II) and La(III) by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent
ce:section.ce:para:
Sorption studies of Pb(II) and La(III) by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was conducted by the microwave sorption technique. A fixed amount of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent (10 mg) was mixed with 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 of ions (Pb(II) or La(III)) and placed in the microwave for 15 s at a constant temperature. The impact of pH study of ions (Pb(II) or La(III)) onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was determined using various pH 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7. The remaining concentration in solution was analyzed by complexometric titration and the amount of adsorbed (Pb(II) or La(III)) by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent is distinguished from Eq. (1).
(1)
q =
C o
-
C r
x V x
10
3
M
( g )
Where Co and Cr are the original and remaining Pb(II) or La(III) concentrations, respectively. V is the volume of aqueous reaction, and M(g) is the mass of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent utilized in gram.

The kinetics and isotherm sorption were investigated by GQDOs-Ba. The evaluated models were discussed in detail in the supplementary materials. The effect of nanobiosorbent dosage was studied by mixing different weights from 10 to 50 mg of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent with 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 of ions (Pb(II) or La(III)) and 9.0 mL of optimum pH at 15 s heating in the microwave oven. Then, the amount of ion (Pb(II) or La(III)) adsorbed by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was then determined according to Eq. (1).

The effect of interfering ions was examined by mixing 10 mg of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent with 100 mg of the coexisting species (KCl, NH4Cl, Na2SO4, CaSO4, and MgSO4) and 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 of ions (Pb(II) or La(III)) and 9.0 mL of the optimum pH at 15 s heating in the microwave oven. The metal capacity of ions (Pb(II) or La(III)) was calculated from Eq. (1).

The application of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent for extraction of Pb(II) and La(III) from two various water samples (industrial wastewater and Tap water) was also investigated. The target specimens were analyzed for Pb(II) and La(III) and spiked with these two ions. A 500 mL water sample was added to 100 ± 1 mg of the GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent and heated in the microwave oven for 15 s. Then, the sample was filtered and examined by ICP to determine the contents of Pb(II) and La(III). The removal percentages of lead (II) or lanthanum (III) were evaluated and recorded according to Eq. (2).
(2)
% R =
C O
-
C F
C o
X 100
Where Co and Cf (mg L−1) are the original and the final concentration of ions Pb(II) or La(III) ions.

The FT-IR spectra of carbon from rice husk, GQDOs, barium hydroxide, and GQDOs-Ba were compared in this study. The FT-IR spectrum of carbon (see Supplementary Data) (Wang et al., 2011, 2007) shows a broad band in the range 3200–3500 cm−1 which is assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds corresponding to alcohol, phenol or adsorbed water. The peak at 1560 cm−1 is referred to the covalent double bond in carbon (CC) as the stretching vibration in the aromatic ring. The peak between 1100 and 1200 cm−1 (1155 cm−1) refers to the stretching of C–O. The peaks at 2668, 2323 and 2093 cm−1 indicate the presence of C–H group. There is no peak around 800 cm−1 to demonstrate the removal of silica in RH ash by using NaOH. The comparison of carbon FT-IR spectrum verified the obvious change in chemical environment from GQDOs. The existence of –OH, CO, C–O, and –COOH bonds indicate that GQDOs are functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups; respectively as represented (see Supplementary Data). The FT-IR spectrum of GQDOs showed several sharp peaks which are referring to oxygen functional groups, viz. 3495 cm−1 (O–H stretching vibration), 1717 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration), and the peaks at 1164 and 1034 cm−1 of C–O vibration (Raeyani et al., 2016; Teymourinia et al., 2017). Besides, the aromatic CC stretching band at 1656 cm−1, which reflects the CC conjugation vibration commonly distinguished in graphene. The peaks at 2876, 2544 and 2459 cm−1 indicate the presence of C–H group. The FT-IR spectrum of barium hydroxide sample (see Supplementary Data) shows strong peaks at 3474 and 3335 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of water molecule and bonded OH stretching, The spectrum also shows a peak at 1621 cm−1 to indicate Ba-OH stretching, and a peak at 1417 cm−1 for the distortion of absorbed water molecules. The peak at 545 and 853 cm−1 are related to the specific vibrations of Ba–O, Ba–O–Ba bonds, respectively (Lutz et al., 1981; Devamani and Alagar, 2014). The FT-IR spectrum of GQDOs-Ba (see Supplementary Data) is completely different from that of barium hydroxide and GQDOs. After modification of GQDOs, new absorption peaks at 2095 and 981 cm−1 are observed for the existence of C–H and C–C bonds, respectively. The peak at 1597 cm−1 was produced instead of the peaks at 1621 and 1750 cm−1 of barium hydroxide as well as 1656 and 1717 cm−1 of GQDOs to confirm the good modification of GQDOs with barium hydroxide to produce GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The new peak at 1064 cm−1 appeared to confirm the combination between GQDOs and barium hydroxide. The peak at 545 cm−1 was shifted to 606 cm−1 to indicated the formation of a new GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The peak at 3396 cm−1 reflects the existence of OH groups on the surface of GQDOs-Ba and the peak at 1064 cm−1 is due to C–O of GQDOs.

The XRD pattern of GQDOs (see Supplementary Data) refers to the amorphous nature of synthesized GQDOs and demonstrates a wide (0 0 2) diffraction peak at 24.98° which is related to graphitic structure. The interlayer dispersing of 0.356 nm was calculated from the XRD concurs well with previously reported values (0.40–0.403 nm) of GQDOs, and slightly higher than 0.34 nm of bulk graphite (Yang et al., 2014). Both the in-plane and basal lattice parameters indicate that the GQDOs exhibit the graphitic structure, not the diamond one. The peaks at 2θ = 22.76°, 24.76°, 25.75°, 26.72°, 28.65°, 31.47°, 32.77° and 42.69° confirm the corresponding peaks of barium hydroxide functionalized GQDOs (see Supplementary Data). The GQDOs plane at 2θ = 24.76° (0 0 2) was not disseminated of barium hydroxide to indicate that the GQDOs properties were retained and the crystalline nature increased by the association with surface loaded barium hydroxide on GQDOs. The modification of GQDOs with barium hydroxide to produce GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent has expanded the d-spacing to 0.37 nm, to confirm the insertion of barium hydroxide in the interlayer between GQDOs layers. Thus, the increased d-spacing and presence of barium hydroxide sites on GQDOs was expected to enhance its dispersion in the aqueous metal ions solution and thus improving its adsorption efficiency.

The morphology of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba as acquired from the SEM analysis is shown (see Supplementary Data). The surface of GQDOs shows relatively smooth large particles. However, the SEM image of GQDOs-Ba (see Supplementary Data) shows better distributed particles than the GQDOs. Almost all particles are spherical in shape to confirm the excellent interaction between GQDOs and barium hydroxide. Furthermore, a good number of pores are observed on the surface of GQDOs-Ba to enhance the adsorption efficiency of this nanobiosorbent.

The HR-TEM images of synthesized GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba are shown with relatively uniform shape and size (see Supplementary Data). The image J and Origin 8 softwares were used to calculate the average particle size of GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba which is found to be in the average 0.2503 ± 0.006 nm and 37.024 ± 0.598 nm for 75 nanoparticles, respectively. This result confirms the coverage of GQDOs by barium hydroxide to form GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent.

Thermal properties of the as-synthesized GQDOs and GQDOs-Ba were investigated. The data are shown (see Supplementary Data) to confirm that GQDs-Ba displayed a high degree of thermal stability. The weight loss of GQDOs at 400 °C is 46.42%, which is referring to the decomposition of stable carboxylic acid groups at the terminal of the GQDOs structure (Vázquez-Nakagawa et al., 2016). The GQDOs skeleton crumbled between 500 and 800 °C. The TGA curve of GQDOs-Ba shows that the main weight loss process occurs at 100–800 °C with a lower decomposition rate compared to GQDOs. In addition, the weight loss of GQDOs-Ba under 400 °C is mainly attributed to thermal conversion of barium hydroxide hydrate into barium hydroxide anhydrous (Habashy and Kolta, 1972). However, above 400 °C the decomposition is related to the dissociation of the carboxylic acid of GQDOs (Vázquez-Nakagawa et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sorption studies of Pb(II) and La(III) by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent

ce:section.ce:para: The surface electrostatic strength of the recently structured GQDOs-Ba is extremely pH-dependent; so its influence on the adsorption procedure was confirmed in the medium using pH 1.0–7.0. The removal results of lead (II) and lanthanum (III) onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent are given in Table 1 . It shows that the adsorption processes of lead (II) and lanthanum (III) ions by GQDOs-Ba are very low in acidic medium and gradually rises up by increasing the pH. This might be attributed on the fact that in very acidic solution, the number of H+ in aqueous solution is higher than the existing ions of lead (II) or lanthanum (III) and thus most carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent are protonated (Mahmoud et al., 2015). In this case, lead (II) and lanthanum (III) ions cannot interact with the hydroxyl groups (–OH) onto the surface of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. However, when the value of pH increases, the dissociation of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDOs-Ba also increase and will be able to directly react with Pb(II) and La(III). After 15 s, the maximum adsorption was attained at pH 5 (1500 µmol g−1 for La (III)) and pH 7 (3400 µmol g−1 for Pb (II)).

For inferring the optimum amount of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent in order to remove both metal ions, the dosage experiment was employed. The dissimilar mass (10–50 mg) levels of the GQDOs-Ba was added to 1 mL of 0.1 M of Pb(II) or La(III) solution and their adsorption capacity was signified in Fig. 1 . The proficiency of GQDOs-Ba for removal of both Pb(II) and La(III) ions was improved with the increase in GQDOs-Ba mass. This can be imputed to the fact that upon the nanobiosorbent dosage increase, the number of active sites increases, which prompts extraordinary accessibility of commutable sites for the evaluated ions. In Fig. 1, the metal capacity value was declined with increasing mass dose. After 15 s, the highest detected values were well-defined as 3350 µmol g−1 and 1500 µmol g−1 using 10 mg of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent for Pb(II) and La(III), respectively. Also, the lowest values of Pb(II) and La(III) were 950 and 510 µmol g−1 using 50 mg of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. So it could be concluded that, for depressed GQDOs-Ba mass, there is a great concentration of Pb(II) or La(III) compared to low active sites on the surface of nanobiosorbent prompting high bonding characters. However, at high GQDOs-Ba mass, the concentrations of both ions are very low compared to the active sites of dosage. The maximum metal capacity was assessed by using 10 mg of GQDOs-Ba dose (Mahmoud et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time and kinetics models
ce:section.ce:para: The Pb(II) and La(III) adsorption on GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent as a function of contact time are shown in Fig. 2 a. It is observed that the qe increased rapidly to achieve 3400 and 1500 µmol g−1 for Pb(II) and La(III), respectively using 15 s of microwave heating. The rapid sorption rates might be attributed to the abundant reacting active sites, which are accessible for Pb(II) and La(III) uptake from aqueous medium. The adsorption capacity of Pb(II) on GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was found higher than La(III). The kinetic reaction of Pb(II) and La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba surface was examined by four different kinetic models (pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, intra-particle diffusion, and Elvoich) (Subha and Namasivayam, 2008). The kinetic parameters were calculated and listed in Table 2 . The pseudo-second-order model curves of Pb(II) and La(III) are shown in Fig. 2b. The high coefficients (R2) of pseudo-second-order were 0.9841 and 1.000 for lead (II) and lanthanum (III), respectively. It is also indicated that the adsorption of Pb(II) and La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba was better simulated by the pseudo-second-order model than other models. At pseudo-second adsorption, the adsorption capacities (qe) were 743.21 and 208.35 mg g−1 for Pb(II) and La(III), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration and adsorption isotherm models
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of initial metal ion concentrations of Pb(ΙΙ) and La(III) on removal by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent were investigated by using 10 mg of GQDOs-Ba with different concentrations at optimum pH and reaction time in the microwave for 15 s at a constant temperature. The interaction of GQDOs-Ba with Pb(II) or La(III) may be interpreted on the basis of various equilibrium isotherms as a very important procedure for designing sorption systems. Sorption isotherms are mainly employed to describe the equilibrium distribution of adsorbed molecules (Pb(II) and La(III)) between the solid (GQDOs-Ba) and liquid phases. Finally, the values of lead (II) and lanthanum (III) will be used to calculate isotherm models.

Fig. 3 shows the adsorption isotherms of Langmuir for lead (II) and lanthanum (III) onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The constants of isotherm models are recorded in Table 3 . As demonstrated, the values of R2 attained by the Langmuir model were (R2 = 0.9991 for lead (II) and R2 = 0.9982 for lanthanum (III)), while those in the case of Freundlich model were found 0.9824 and 0.9656 for lead (II) and lanthanum (III), respectively. In the Langmuir model, the qmax values of Pb(II) and La(III) were corresponding to 748.55 and 207.38 mg g−1, respectively. These qmax values are very near to the investigational experimental metal capacity results to confirm that the Langmuir model gave an excellent example for the adsorption of both ions onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The values of RL were identified between 0 and 1 for both ions to indicate favorable adsorption of Pb(II) and La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba surface. According to the R2 values, the results of lanthanum and lead ions adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent were fitted into Langmuir isotherm, demonstrating that monolayer coverage were the fundamental adsorption mechanism, which is reliable with the strong chemical binding between lead (II) or lanthanum (III) ions and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surfaces of GQDOs-Ba. In addition, the adsorption capacity of Pb(II) on GQDOs-Ba was much higher than that of La(III), which is consistent with the aforementioned results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature and thermodynamic study
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of temperature on Pb(II) and La(III) removal by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent was also investigated and demonstrated. The metal capacity of Lead (II) increased with an increase of temperature but in the case of lanthanum (III) decreased with increased temperature. The thermodynamic parameters (ΔG°, ΔH°, and ΔS°) were investigated from the T dependent adsorption. The calculated values of ΔG° can be assessed (see Supplementary Data). The values of ΔH° and ΔS° were obtained from the slope and intercept of plot ln kd against 1/T via the Van't Hoff (see Supplementary Data) and the calculated values are recorded in Table 4 . The negative ΔG° values of Pb(II) and La(III) adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba indicate that the adsorption reactions were based on spontaneous processes. The negative ΔG° values for Pb(II) and La(III) were increased from −5.24 to −11.02 and −7.47 to −8.14, with the rise of temperature from 291 to 353 K, respectively. This trend confirms the practicability of adsorption procedure onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The ΔH° value of Pb(II) was positive (21.73 KJ mol−1) to refer that the adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba was accompanied by the acquisition of heat. This meant that the adsorption of Pb(II) on GQDOs-Ba was endothermic. The ΔH° negative value of La(III) onto GQDOs-Ba (−4.36 KJ mol−1) indicates that the adsorption route was enhanced by the progression of heat, which means that the adsorption reaction is exothermic. The values of ΔH° for both ions were in the range 2.1–20.9 KJ mol−1 to reveal that the adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba was physical (Sheela et al., 2012). The positive ΔS° values of Pb(II) and La(III) confirm that the disorder degree increases during the reaction procedures of the investigated two ions by GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent in addition to the alteration in power distribution among adsorption and self-adsorption (Nagarai et al., 2018).

Adsorption capacity of lead (II) and lanthanum (III) ions in the presence of foreign ions was studied by the synthesized GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent at optimum pH, with (0.1 mol L−1) of Pb (II) or La (III) ions using 100 mg of the interfering compounds such as (KCl, NH4Cl, Na2SO4, CaSO4 and MgSO4) at equilibrium contact time. The interfering ions were found to exhibit either no effect, decrease or improve the sorption capacity value depending on the type of interfering ion, GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent and adsorbed metal ion (Pb(II) and La(III)). Fig. 4 shows the results of the competing ions on the metal adsorption capacity by the synthesized GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. The presence of NH4 + in NH4Cl, Ca(II) in CaSO4, Mg(II) in MgSO4 and Na(I) in Na2SO4 were identified to slightly improve the metal capacity values of La(III) from 1500 to 1800, 1550, 1600 and 1700 μmol g−1, respectively. In addition, the sorption capacity of La(III) using GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent in presence of K+ in KCl ions were found to show strong interference by producing 2150 μmol g−1, that may be regarded to the size of potassium (K+) ions and selectivity of lanthanum (III) from solution (Mahmoud et al., 2018). The competing cations such as ions K+ and NH4 + significantly decreased Pb(II) adsorption onto GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent. However, it was found that Ca(II) in CaSO4 (4500 μmol g−1), Mg+2 in MgSO4 (5000 μmol g−1) and Na(I) in Na2SO4 (7200 μmol g−1) are causing strong interference behaviors compared to K+ and NH4 +.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications for extraction of Pb(II) and La(III) from real water
ce:section.ce:para: The applications of GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent for recovery and removal of Pb(II) and La(III) ions from various waters were also implemented using the microwave extraction process. In this work, 100.0 ± 1.0 mg mass of GQDOs-Ba was utilized for removal of the spiked 5 mg L−1 of Pb(II) and La(III). The extraction percentages determined by the microwave extraction of Pb(II) from tap water and industrial wastewater samples were characterized as 99.8% and 98.5%, respectively, while those for La(III) were 96.2%, 94.6% respectively, using the second extraction step. It is indicated that the GQDOs-Ba acts as an excellent nanobiosorbent for removal Pb(II) and La(III).

Based on the collected results from this study, one can state that the designed and evaluated GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent has been acted is an excellent low cost sustainable material for removal of Pb(II) and La(III) from aqueous solutions under the influence of microwave irradiation for short reaction times. The estimated maximum sample volume can be regarded in the range of 5–10 L which is mainly dependent on the capacity of microwave oven. However, the sample volume may be expanded to higher values if column technique is used. Finally, other GQDOs derivatives may be designed and assembled for potential applications in removal of other organic and inorganic pollutants from various matrices.

The reported results in this work confirm a successful procedure for GQDOs manufacturing with 2D morphology from rice husk as a sustainable source. GQDOs were additionally functionalized with Ba(OH)2 to produce a novel GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent with excellent stability in aqueous solution and high thermal stability between 50 and 800 °C. GQDOs-Ba nanobiosorbent exhibited excellent capability of microwave sorption and binding with Pb(II) and La(III) ions at 15 s. The kinetics and isotherm were fitted with pseudo-second-order and Langmuir models. The thermodynamics were fitted with spontaneous reactions. The extraction of these ions from different water samples were successfully accomplished using microwave sorption technique.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced sulfate and metal removal by reduced graphene oxide self-assembled Enterococcus avium sulfate-reducing bacteria particles
